symbols: orb with cross — Christ's universal sovereignty over all creation
raised hand in blessing — the divine benediction bestowed upon the faithful
jeweled crown — Christ as King of Kings, ruler of heaven and earth
flanking angels with books — the heavenly choir singing praise and recording the deeds of the faithful
radiant halo — the uncreated divine light emanating from the Son of God
description: A horizontal panel painting showing Christ enthroned at center in a frontal pose, wearing a dark robe decorated with golden circular ornaments or gemstones. He wears a tall, elaborate jeweled crown and has long brown hair and a short beard. His right hand is raised in a gesture of blessing while his left holds a crystal orb surmounted by a cross — the Salvator Mundi globe. Behind his head, a large radiant halo or mandorla emanates golden light. On either side, three or four angels with large dark wings stand in attendance, some holding open books, their faces turned toward Christ.
theological_description: This Christ Blessing or Salvator Mundi panel presents the risen and glorified Christ in his cosmic authority. The crystal orb surmounted by a cross is a visual confession that all creation belongs to Christ: 'For by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities — all things were created through him and for him' (Colossians 1:16). The blessing gesture fulfills the Aaronic benediction in its ultimate form: 'The Lord bless you and keep you; the Lord make his face to shine upon you' (Numbers 6:24-25) — here it is the Lord himself whose face shines and whose hand blesses. The jeweled crown identifies Christ as the King of Kings and Lord of Lords (Revelation 19:16), fulfilling the prophecy of Psalm 2:6: 'I have set my King on Zion, my holy hill.' The flanking angels with books recall the heavenly liturgy of Revelation, where the elders and living creatures worship the Lamb who is worthy to open the scroll (Revelation 5:9-12). Memling's precise, gem-like rendering of every jewel, fold, and feather is itself a theological act: the glory of the risen Christ exceeds all earthly beauty, and the artist's utmost skill still falls short of the reality it represents.

scripture_refs.reason: Christ as the image of the invisible God through whom all things were created — the orb of universal sovereignty
King of Kings and Lord of Lords — the crowned Christ in majestic authority
The divine blessing gesture fulfills the priestly benediction in the person of Christ himself
Myriads of angels surrounding the throne singing praise, depicted by the flanking angels with books
God setting his King on Zion, fulfilled in the crowned and enthroned Christ

_meta.title: Christ Blessing, central panel from a triptych from the Church of Santa Maria la Real, Najera
